From Chroma to FAISS: A Simple Guide to Vector Databases with LangChain

Yusup Hidayat Winata
4 min read
·
Jun 5, 2025

In my previous article, I walked through how to build a vector database using LangChain and Chroma to support semantic search and retrieval tasks. This time, I want to explore an alternative backend: FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search).

Both Chroma and FAISS are powerful tools for building vector databases, but they serve slightly different needs. Chroma is known for its simplicity and user-friendly setup — especially great for rapid prototyping and in-browser use. FAISS, on the other hand, is a high-performance library developed by Facebook AI that’s optimized for fast, large-scale similarity search. It supports both CPU and GPU acceleration and is well-suited for production-level applications that deal with millions of vectors efficiently.

In terms of workflow, the process of preparing and embedding documents is nearly identical to the Chroma-based approach. So in this short article, I’ll focus on the key code differences needed to build a vector store using FAISS with LangChain. You’ll still be able to perform semantic retrieval and visualize the vector space in 2D and 3D using the same tools and methods.

As before, the dataset I use is hosted in the same GitHub repository:

https://github.com/yusupwinata/Basic-VectorDB

Let’s dive in!

Step 1: Install FAISS and Import Required Packages

🔧 Installation

For Python versions > 3.10, it’s recommended to use the CPU version:

pip install faiss-cpu

If you’re working on a system with a supported GPU and compatible Python version (≤ 3.10), you can install the GPU-accelerated version:

pip install faiss-gpu

💡 Tip: If you’re unsure or just testing things out, starting with faiss-cpu is the safest and simplest choice.

📦 Import FAISS into Your Project

Once installed, you can import the FAISS vector store implementation from LangChain:

from langchain.vectorstores import FAISS

Now you’re ready to move forward and build your FAISS-powered vector database.

Step 2: Choose Your Embedding Model

The process of chunking the knowledge base remains unchanged from the previous article, so we’ll jump directly into building the vector database.

Before creating the FAISS index, you need to choose the embedding model that will convert your text chunks into numerical vectors. You have two great options:

🔑 Option 1: OpenAI Embeddings

A paid, cloud-based solution offering high-quality and fast embeddings. Ideal for production use if you already have an OpenAI API key.

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from dotenv import load_dotenv
import os

load_dotenv(override=True)
OPENAI_API_KEY = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY", "type-your-api-key-here")

db_name = DB_PATH
openai_embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()

💡 Option 2: HuggingFace Embeddings

A free, local option using models from the Hugging Face Hub. Great for experimentation and local development.

from huggingface_hub import login
from langchain_huggingface import HuggingFaceEmbeddings

HF_TOKEN = os.getenv("HUGGINGFACE_TOKEN", "type-your-token-here")
login(HF_TOKEN)

db_name = DB_HF_PATH
hf_embeddings = HuggingFaceEmbeddings(model="sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

🙌 Feel free to use either option based on your needs. Both will work seamlessly with FAISS in the next step.

Step 3: Build the FAISS Vector Database

Once you’ve chosen your embedding model and generated chunks from the knowledge base, it’s time to build the FAISS vector store.

But first, let’s make sure we’re not accidentally overwriting an existing database.

🧹 Check for Existing FAISS Database

Before creating a new vector store, check if a FAISS index already exists locally and clean it up if needed:

import os
import shutil

if os.path.exists(DB_PATH): # or use DB_HF_PATH
    print("FAISS vectorstore already exists. Delete FAISS vectorstore from directory.")
    shutil.rmtree(DB_PATH)

🏗️ Create and Save the FAISS Vector Store

Now, let’s build the actual vector database using your selected embedding model:

vectorstore = FAISS.from_documents(
    documents=chunks,  # your chunked documents
    embedding=openai_embeddings  # or use hf_embeddings
)

# Save the FAISS vectorstore manually
vectorstore.save_local(DB_PATH)

📊 Vector Summary

To verify what’s inside the FAISS index:

total_vectors = vectorstore.index.ntotal
dimensions = vectorstore.index.d
print(f"There are {total_vectors} vectors with {dimensions:,} dimensions in this FAISS vector store.")

📝 Tip: You can freely swap between openai_embeddings or hf_embeddings, and adjust the DB_PATH as needed to manage different versions or environments.

Step 4: Simple Retrieval Example from FAISS

# Sampling a single vector inside the FAISS vectorstore

def sampling_vector(vectorstore: FAISS, vector_index: int) -> None:
    # Get document ID linked to vector index
    doc_id = vectorstore.index_to_docstore_id[vector_index]
    
    # Search in docstore using the ID
    document = vectorstore.docstore.search(doc_id)  # contains page_content & metadata
    print(f"Vector:\n{document}\n\n")
    
    # Retrieve the actual vector values (embedding)
    sample_vector = vectorstore.index.reconstruct(vector_index)
    print(f"This vector has {len(sample_vector)} dimensions:\n{sample_vector[:25]}")  # show first 25 dims

sampling_vector(vectorstore=vectorstore, vector_index=0)

This gives you both:

  • The original chunk of text and its metadata stored in the vectorstore.
  • A sample of the embedding values generated from that chunk.

📝 Note: You can use either OpenAIEmbeddings or HuggingFaceEmbeddings—the retrieval logic remains the same.

Final Thoughts

In this article, we explored how to build a vector database using FAISS, a powerful and efficient similarity search library favored for its speed and scalability. While FAISS and Chroma share many similarities in how you create and manage vector stores, FAISS is often preferred when working with large datasets locally or requiring highly optimized search.

Remember, the chunking and embedding steps remain consistent regardless of your vector store choice, allowing you to switch tools based on your project needs or resource constraints.

Visualization techniques covered previously can also be applied to FAISS, helping you better understand your vector space and document distribution.

As always, the goal is to provide practical, accessible methods for building retrieval-augmented systems, and I hope this continuation helps deepen your understanding of vector databases.

Feel free to experiment with embeddings, chunk sizes, and vector stores to find what best suits your applications.

Thank you for following along — happy building!
